questions.prompt: What does the opening line of the Aeneid — 'Arma virumque cano, Troiae qui primus ab oris' (Aeneid 1.1) — mean?
choices: I sing of arms and the man, who first from the shores of Troy.
I praise the weapons of the men who fought at Troy.
I tell of the wars and the heroes of the Trojan shore.
I sing of armies and citizens who came to Troy.
questions.correctText: I sing of arms and the man, who first from the shores of Troy.
questions.explanation: 'Arma' = arms (acc. pl.), 'virum' = man (acc. sg., refers to Aeneas), '-que' = and, 'cano' = I sing. 'Troiae … ab oris' = 'from the shores of Troy.' This is the programmatic opening that announces both epic warfare (arma, echoing the Iliad) and a single heroic protagonist (virum, echoing the Odyssey).

questions.prompt: In Aeneid Book 1, what is Juno's principal motive for hating the Trojans and persecuting Aeneas?
choices: She lost the Judgment of Paris to Venus and fears Trojan descendants will one day destroy her beloved Carthage.
She is angry that Aeneas refused to marry her daughter.
She seeks revenge for the death of her son in the Trojan War.
She wishes to prevent the founding of Athens by Trojan exiles.
questions.correctText: She lost the Judgment of Paris to Venus and fears Trojan descendants will one day destroy her beloved Carthage.
questions.explanation: Vergil opens Book 1 by enumerating Juno's grievances: the Judgment of Paris (which honored Venus over her), her favoritism toward Carthage, and the prophecy that Trojan offspring (Rome) will one day overthrow it. These drive her to ask Aeolus to unleash the storm.

questions.prompt: Why is Aeneas repeatedly called 'pius' throughout the Aeneid (e.g., 'pius Aeneas')?
choices: Because he is exceptionally religious in a Christian sense.
Because he embodies pietas — dutiful devotion to gods, family, and fatherland.
Because he avoids combat whenever possible.
Because he is chaste and refuses all romantic relationships.
questions.correctText: Because he embodies pietas — dutiful devotion to gods, family, and fatherland.
questions.explanation: Roman pietas means proper duty to the gods (numen), one's family (especially parents and children), and the patria. Aeneas carries his father Anchises from burning Troy, leads his son Iulus, and obeys the gods' command to found a new city — the trifecta of Roman pietas.

questions.prompt: In De Bello Gallico Book 4, Caesar famously bridges the Rhine in just ten days to overawe the Germani. After his brief crossing and demonstration, what does he do with this remarkable bridge?
choices: He garrisons it permanently with two legions.
He destroys it immediately upon his return to Gaul, having made his point.
He sells it to a friendly Gallic tribe for tribute.
He leaves it intact and continues the campaign east of the Rhine.
questions.correctText: He destroys it immediately upon his return to Gaul, having made his point.
questions.explanation: Caesar's Rhine bridge — described in elaborate engineering detail in BG 4.17 — was a propaganda statement as much as a military one: Rome could reach into Germania at will. After only eighteen days east of the Rhine, Caesar returns to Gaul and tears down the bridge, refusing to leave a permanent foothold but having demonstrated Roman engineering supremacy.

questions.prompt: Who is Laocoön in Aeneid Book 2, and what is his fate?
choices: A Greek warrior who hides inside the Trojan Horse and is killed by Aeneas.
A Trojan priest who warns against the horse and is killed (with his two sons) by sea serpents.
A Trojan king who orders the horse to be burned.
A Greek prophet who foretells the fall of Troy.
questions.correctText: A Trojan priest who warns against the horse and is killed (with his two sons) by sea serpents.
questions.explanation: Laocoön famously warns 'timeo Danaos et dona ferentis' ('I fear the Greeks even bearing gifts,' 2.49) and hurls a spear at the horse. Two enormous serpents emerge from the sea and crush him and his sons — the Trojans take this as a sign to bring the horse inside, sealing their doom.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'urbs antiqua ruit' (Aeneid 2.363), describing Troy on the night it falls.
choices: An ancient city is built.
An ancient city is falling.
An ancient city burns brightly.
An ancient city is rebuilt.
questions.correctText: An ancient city is falling.
questions.explanation: 'urbs' = city (nom. sg. fem.), 'antiqua' = ancient (nom. sg. fem. adjective agreeing with urbs), 'ruit' = is falling / collapses (3rd sg. present active of 'ruo'). The historical present heightens the immediacy of Troy's destruction.

questions.prompt: How does Aeneas escape from burning Troy, and what does this scene illustrate about his character?
choices: He fights his way out alone, abandoning his family — illustrating his warrior pride.
He carries his aged father Anchises on his shoulders, leads his son Iulus by the hand, and his wife Creusa follows — illustrating his pietas.
He swims across the harbor to a Greek ship — illustrating his cunning.
He is rescued by Venus in a divine chariot — illustrating divine favor alone.
questions.correctText: He carries his aged father Anchises on his shoulders, leads his son Iulus by the hand, and his wife Creusa follows — illustrating his pietas.
questions.explanation: This iconic image — Aeneas with Anchises (carrying the household gods, penates) on his back and little Iulus at his hand — became the visual shorthand for Roman pietas (devotion across generations). Creusa is lost in the chaos and later appears to him as a shade with a prophecy.

questions.prompt: In Aeneid Book 4, where do Dido and Aeneas consummate their relationship, and what staged this moment?
choices: At Dido's banquet, after a long evening of wine.
In a cave during a thunderstorm on a royal hunt — engineered by Juno and Venus.
On Aeneas's ship as they sailed together.
In the temple of Juno during a sacrifice.
questions.correctText: In a cave during a thunderstorm on a royal hunt — engineered by Juno and Venus.
questions.explanation: Juno (wanting to keep Aeneas at Carthage) and Venus (wanting her son safe) collude to stage a storm during a hunt. Dido and Aeneas take shelter in a cave, where their union takes place. Dido calls it marriage; Aeneas does not — the ambiguity drives the rest of Book 4.

questions.prompt: When Mercury delivers Jupiter's command that Aeneas must leave Carthage and Dido learns of his preparations, how does she respond before her death?
choices: She forgives him and wishes him well on his voyage to Italy.
She curses Aeneas and all his descendants, prophesying eternal war between Carthage and Rome.
She follows him secretly aboard his ship.
She returns peacefully to her former kingdom of Tyre.
questions.correctText: She curses Aeneas and all his descendants, prophesying eternal war between Carthage and Rome.
questions.explanation: Dido's dying curse — calling on an avenger to rise from her bones ('exoriare aliquis nostris ex ossibus ultor,' 4.625) — etiologically anticipates Hannibal and the Punic Wars. She then climbs the pyre and falls on Aeneas's sword, becoming a tragic icon of love destroyed by duty.

questions.prompt: What is the 'golden bough' (aureus ramus) in Aeneid Book 6, and what is its function?
choices: A weapon Aeneas wields against Charon.
A magical talisman that grants Aeneas safe passage into and out of the underworld.
A scepter symbolizing Aeneas's future kingship in Italy.
A wreath given to him by the Sibyl as a trophy.
questions.correctText: A magical talisman that grants Aeneas safe passage into and out of the underworld.
questions.explanation: The Sibyl of Cumae tells Aeneas he must pluck the golden bough as a gift for Proserpina before descending to the underworld. Two doves (sent by his mother Venus) guide him to it. He presents it to Charon and later to Proserpina, securing passage to and from the realm of the dead.

questions.prompt: In Anchises's prophecy in the underworld (Book 6), what is the central mission Anchises tells Aeneas the Romans will have, summarized in the famous lines 'tu regere imperio populos, Romane, memento … parcere subiectis et debellare superbos' (6.851-853)?
choices: To master the arts, sciences, and oratory above all peoples.
To rule the nations with authority, sparing the conquered and crushing the proud.
To explore unknown lands and trade with distant peoples.
To preserve the religion of their Trojan ancestors unchanged.
questions.correctText: To rule the nations with authority, sparing the conquered and crushing the proud.
questions.explanation: Anchises grants the arts to Greeks and the sciences to others, but Romans are given the imperial mission: 'remember to rule peoples with authority' ('regere imperio populos … memento'), 'to spare the conquered' ('parcere subiectis'), 'and to war down the proud' ('debellare superbos'). This is Vergil's poetic charter for the Augustan empire.

questions.prompt: The famous opening of De Bello Gallico — 'Gallia est omnis divisa in partes tres' (BG 1.1) — means what?
choices: Gaul is now united into one province.
All Gaul is divided into three parts.
Gaul has three principal rivers.
Gaul produces three kinds of crops.
questions.correctText: All Gaul is divided into three parts.
questions.explanation: 'Gallia' (Gaul, nom. sg.) 'est … divisa' (is divided, pf. pass. with est) 'omnis' (all, agreeing with Gallia) 'in partes tres' (into three parts). Caesar then names the three: the Belgae, the Aquitani, and those who in their own tongue are called Celtae, in ours Galli.

questions.prompt: Who is Orgetorix, and what does he persuade the Helvetii to do at the start of Book 1?
choices: A Roman general who orders the Helvetii to settle in northern Italy.
A wealthy Helvetian nobleman who persuades his people to leave their territory and migrate west through Gaul.
A Druid priest who calls for war against Caesar.
A Gallic king who allies with Caesar against the Germani.
questions.correctText: A wealthy Helvetian nobleman who persuades his people to leave their territory and migrate west through Gaul.
questions.explanation: Orgetorix, described as 'longe nobilissimus et ditissimus' (by far the noblest and richest), conspires with neighboring nobles (Casticus and Dumnorix) for tripartite domination of Gaul and convinces the Helvetii to burn their towns and migrate. When his plot is uncovered, he dies before the migration begins, but the migration goes forward.

questions.prompt: How does Caesar block the Helvetii's intended migration route through the Roman Province?
choices: He destroys the bridge across the Rhone at Geneva and builds a long fortified wall.
He bribes neighboring tribes to attack them.
He orders the Helvetii to relocate to Britain.
He grants them passage in exchange for tribute.
questions.correctText: He destroys the bridge across the Rhone at Geneva and builds a long fortified wall.
questions.explanation: Caesar cuts the bridge at Genava (Geneva) and constructs a wall (murus) about nineteen Roman miles long from Lake Lemannus (Geneva) to the Jura range, then refuses the Helvetii's request to pass — forcing them onto the harder northern route, where he ultimately defeats them at Bibracte.

questions.prompt: What is Caesar's stated reason in De Bello Gallico Book 4 for his first crossing into Britain?
choices: To establish a permanent Roman province in Britain.
Because the Britons had aided the Gauls in nearly every Gallic campaign against him.
To rescue Roman merchants held captive there.
To search for tin and silver mines.
questions.correctText: Because the Britons had aided the Gauls in nearly every Gallic campaign against him.
questions.explanation: Caesar writes that 'in omnibus fere Gallicis bellis hostibus nostris … auxilia missa' — auxiliaries had been sent to his enemies from Britain in nearly all the Gallic wars. The first expedition (55 BCE) is more a reconnaissance and demonstration than a conquest, and it nearly ends in disaster when storms wreck many of his ships at anchor.

questions.prompt: When Caesar's legions hesitate to leap from the ships into the British surf against hostile cavalry and chariots, what action turns the tide?
choices: Caesar himself jumps into the water and leads the charge.
The standard-bearer (aquilifer) of the Tenth Legion leaps into the sea with the eagle, shaming the soldiers into following.
A sudden storm scatters the British forces.
Roman cavalry arrives unexpectedly from another ship.
questions.correctText: The standard-bearer (aquilifer) of the Tenth Legion leaps into the sea with the eagle, shaming the soldiers into following.
questions.explanation: In one of the most cinematic moments in BG, the aquilifer of Legio X jumps overboard carrying the legionary eagle, calling on his comrades not to let the eagle be captured by the enemy. The legionaries, unwilling to bear that disgrace, plunge in after him and force the landing.

questions.prompt: Which Briton becomes the principal organizer of resistance against Caesar's second invasion of Britain?
choices: Vercingetorix
Cassivellaunus
Ambiorix
Boudicca
questions.correctText: Cassivellaunus
questions.explanation: Caesar reports that supreme command of the British forces was given to Cassivellaunus, whose territory lay north of the Thames. Cassivellaunus uses guerrilla tactics with chariots and ultimately negotiates a peace after Caesar storms his stronghold.

questions.prompt: In the winter following the second British invasion, Sabinus and Cotta — Roman legates wintering in Eburones territory — are tricked and destroyed. Who is the chieftain responsible for this disaster?
choices: Vercingetorix of the Arverni
Ambiorix of the Eburones
Cassivellaunus of the Britons
Orgetorix of the Helvetii
questions.correctText: Ambiorix of the Eburones
questions.explanation: Ambiorix persuades Sabinus and Cotta to abandon their winter camp, promising safe passage; he then ambushes the column and annihilates roughly a legion and a half — one of the worst Roman defeats in the Gallic Wars. Caesar spends the rest of BG 5 and beyond hunting Ambiorix, who eludes him.

questions.prompt: In Caesar's ethnographic digression in Book 6, what are the TWO chief social classes among the Gauls 'qui aliquo sunt numero atque honore' (who count for anything in rank and honor)?
choices: Senators and tribunes
Druids and equites (knights)
Merchants and farmers
Kings and slaves
questions.correctText: Druids and equites (knights)
questions.explanation: Caesar writes that the common people are treated almost as slaves; the two classes 'of any account' are the Druids (who handle religion and law) and the equites (who fight in war). The plebs is reduced to dependence on one of these two.

questions.prompt: Which striking sacrificial practice does Caesar attribute to the Gauls in Book 6, particularly when life is in danger?
choices: Sacrificing pure white bulls to Mars.
Burning human beings alive inside enormous wicker figures (often called 'wicker men').
Offering grain and wine at every full moon.
Casting prisoners into the Rhine river.
questions.correctText: Burning human beings alive inside enormous wicker figures (often called 'wicker men').
questions.explanation: Caesar reports that the Gauls build immense figures (immani magnitudine simulacra) woven of twigs, fill the limbs with living people, set them on fire, and burn the victims as a sacrifice — preferring criminals, but using the innocent when criminals are short. The 'wicker man' image enters Western imagination from this passage.

questions.prompt: Identify the case and use of 'Italiam' in 'Italiam fato profugus … venit' (Aeneid 1.2-3 — 'driven by fate he came to Italy').
choices: Ablative of place where
Accusative of place to which (with names of countries, often without preposition in poetry)
Genitive of possession
Dative of indirect object
questions.correctText: Accusative of place to which (with names of countries, often without preposition in poetry)
questions.explanation: 'Italiam' is accusative singular feminine of Italia. Although standard prose normally uses 'ad' or 'in' + accusative for 'to a country,' Vergil (and Latin poetry generally) freely omits the preposition with country names. With city names and small islands, the preposition is omitted even in prose.

questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly lists the FOUR principal parts of the verb meaning 'to carry / to bear'?
choices: fero, ferre, ferebam, latus
fero, ferre, tuli, latum
ferre, ferens, ferentis, ferendum
porto, portare, portavi, portatum
questions.correctText: fero, ferre, tuli, latum
questions.explanation: 'Fero' is irregular: 1st sg. present 'fero,' present infinitive 'ferre,' perfect 'tuli,' and perfect passive participle 'latum.' (The fourth option, porto/portare/portavi/portatum, is a perfectly regular 1st-conjugation verb that also means 'to carry,' but is a different word.)

questions.prompt: Translate the tense and voice of 'amabatur': he/she/it ____.
choices: is loved (present passive)
was loved / used to be loved (imperfect passive)
had been loved (pluperfect passive)
will be loved (future passive)
questions.correctText: was loved / used to be loved (imperfect passive)
questions.explanation: The stem 'ama-' + imperfect marker '-ba-' + passive ending '-tur' = 'amabatur,' imperfect passive 3rd singular: 'he/she/it was being loved' or 'used to be loved.' The imperfect signals continuous or habitual past action.

questions.prompt: Which Latin participle expresses ACTIVE past action — 'having (already) done something' — and is found on only deponent verbs?
choices: Present active participle (e.g., amans, amantis)
Future active participle (e.g., amaturus, -a, -um)
Perfect active participle, found only on deponents (e.g., locutus = 'having spoken')
Perfect passive participle (e.g., amatus, -a, -um)
questions.correctText: Perfect active participle, found only on deponents (e.g., locutus = 'having spoken')
questions.explanation: Ordinary Latin verbs lack a perfect active participle (a major gap). Deponent verbs — passive in form, active in meaning — fill the gap: 'locutus' (from loquor) means 'having spoken,' actively. This is why deponents are so useful in narrative.

questions.prompt: Translate the ablative absolute 'urbe capta': '____, the army withdrew.'
choices: Before the city was captured
With the city captured / Once the city had been captured
While the city is being captured
Because of the city's capture
questions.correctText: With the city captured / Once the city had been captured
questions.explanation: An ablative absolute is a participial phrase in the ablative, grammatically independent from the main clause. 'urbs' (city) + 'capta' (perfect passive participle, fem. nom./abl.) → 'urbe capta' (abl. abs.) = literally 'the city having been captured,' or naturally, 'once the city had been captured.'

questions.prompt: Translate the indirect statement: 'Caesar dicit hostes venire.'
choices: Caesar tells the enemies to come.
Caesar says that the enemies are coming.
Caesar comes to speak with the enemies.
Caesar says, 'Enemies, come!'
questions.correctText: Caesar says that the enemies are coming.
questions.explanation: In Latin indirect statement, the subject of the reported clause is in the accusative ('hostes') and the verb is an infinitive ('venire,' present active infinitive). 'Caesar says that X is happening' — the present infinitive expresses an action contemporaneous with the main verb.

questions.prompt: What kind of subordinate clause is 'ut hostes vinceret' in 'Caesar venit ut hostes vinceret' ('Caesar came in order to conquer the enemy')?
choices: Result clause (ut + subjunctive)
Purpose clause (ut + subjunctive)
Indirect command
Conditional clause
questions.correctText: Purpose clause (ut + subjunctive)
questions.explanation: 'ut' + subjunctive expresses purpose: 'in order to / so that he might conquer.' Result clauses also use ut + subjunctive but typically have a 'so/such' word (tam, ita, tantus, talis) in the main clause and translate as '(so that as a result).' Negative purpose uses 'ne,' negative result uses 'ut non.'

questions.prompt: In the sentence 'tantus erat clamor ut omnes audirent' ('so great was the shout that everyone heard'), what kind of clause is 'ut omnes audirent'?
choices: Purpose clause
Result clause
Indirect statement
Temporal clause
questions.correctText: Result clause
questions.explanation: The trigger word 'tantus' ('so great') signals an expected result clause. 'ut omnes audirent' = 'that all were hearing / could hear.' Distinguishing purpose vs. result: result clauses negate with 'ut non,' purpose with 'ne,' and result clauses typically follow tam/ita/tantus/talis/sic in the main clause.

questions.prompt: Identify the type and translate this conditional sentence: 'Si veniat, gaudeam.'
choices: Simple present: 'If he comes, I am glad.'
Future more vivid: 'If he comes, I shall be glad.'
Future less vivid (should-would): 'If he should come, I would be glad.'
Contrary to fact present: 'If he were coming, I would be glad.'
questions.correctText: Future less vivid (should-would): 'If he should come, I would be glad.'
questions.explanation: Both verbs are present subjunctive ('veniat,' 'gaudeam'). Present subjunctive in both protasis (si-clause) and apodosis (main clause) = future less vivid (also called 'should-would'). Contrary-to-fact uses imperfect (present) or pluperfect (past) subjunctive.

questions.prompt: In 'puella rosas pulchras dat' (the girl gives beautiful roses), what is the case and number of 'rosas pulchras'?
choices: Nominative plural — subject of the verb
Accusative plural — direct object of the verb
Genitive plural — possession
Dative singular — indirect object
questions.correctText: Accusative plural — direct object of the verb
questions.explanation: 'Rosas' is the accusative plural of 'rosa' (1st declension). 'Pulchras' is the matching feminine accusative plural of 'pulcher, -chra, -chrum.' Adjectives must agree with their nouns in gender, number, and case. They are the direct object of 'dat' ('she gives').

questions.prompt: The English word 'belligerent' derives from which Latin word(s)?
choices: 'Bellus' (pretty) + '-gerent' (carrying)
'Bellum' (war) + 'gero' (to wage/carry on) — literally 'war-waging'
'Bellator' (warrior) alone
'Bella' (beautiful things, neut. pl.) + a Greek suffix
questions.correctText: 'Bellum' (war) + 'gero' (to wage/carry on) — literally 'war-waging'
questions.explanation: The Latin idiom 'bellum gerere' means 'to wage war' (gero = carry, conduct). 'Belligerent' literally means 'war-waging' — hence aggressive or eager to fight. Note: 'bellum' (war, neuter) is a different word from 'bellus' (pretty, adjective), which gives us 'belle' and 'embellish.'

questions.prompt: Which of these English words does NOT derive from a Latin root?
choices: Annual (from 'annus,' year)
Magnify (from 'magnus,' great + facio)
Sister (cognate with Latin 'soror' but inherited via Germanic, not borrowed from Latin)
Liberate (from 'liber,' free)
questions.correctText: Sister (cognate with Latin 'soror' but inherited via Germanic, not borrowed from Latin)
questions.explanation: 'Sister' comes from Old English 'sweostor' from Proto-Germanic *swestēr — cognate (sharing a common Proto-Indo-European ancestor) with Latin 'soror,' but inherited natively, not borrowed from Latin. Borrowed Latin-derived 'soror-' words in English include 'sorority' and 'sororal.' The other three are direct Latin borrowings.

questions.prompt: What does the verb 'navigare' mean, and what familiar English word descends from it?
choices: 'To explore'; English 'explore'
'To sail / to navigate'; English 'navigate' and 'navy'
'To swim'; English 'natation'
'To row'; English 'remit'
questions.correctText: 'To sail / to navigate'; English 'navigate' and 'navy'
questions.explanation: 'Navigare' = 'to sail,' from 'navis' (ship) + 'ago' (drive). English 'navigate,' 'navy,' 'naval,' and 'navigation' all descend from this family. ('Natare' = 'to swim,' from which 'natation,' a different root.)

questions.prompt: In Caesar's military narratives, 'oppidum' most precisely refers to which of the following?
choices: A Roman colony established by veterans.
A fortified Gallic or non-Roman town (often hill-fort), as distinct from a Roman 'urbs' or 'colonia.'
A temporary marching camp built nightly.
A coastal trading post.
questions.correctText: A fortified Gallic or non-Roman town (often hill-fort), as distinct from a Roman 'urbs' or 'colonia.'
questions.explanation: Caesar uses 'oppidum' for fortified non-Roman towns — typically Gallic hill-forts (like Avaricum, Gergovia, Alesia in Book 7). 'Urbs' (especially with the article, Urbs = Rome itself) and 'colonia' are reserved for Roman foundations. 'Castra' is a military camp.

questions.prompt: Vergil frequently uses 'fata' (neuter plural of 'fatum'). What is its core meaning, and from what verb does it derive?
choices: 'Voices'; from 'voco' (to call)
'Fates / the things spoken / divine decrees'; from 'for, fari' (to speak)
'Fortunes'; from 'fortuno' (to grant good luck)
'Stars'; from 'sidus' (constellation)
questions.correctText: 'Fates / the things spoken / divine decrees'; from 'for, fari' (to speak)
questions.explanation: 'Fatum' is the neuter perfect passive participle of 'for, fari' ('to speak') — literally 'a thing spoken,' i.e., a divine utterance or decree. Hence 'the fates' are 'what has been spoken' by the gods. English 'fate,' 'fatal,' and 'fateful' all descend from this. The same root gives 'infant' (in- + fans, 'not speaking').

questions.prompt: Which of the following best defines Roman 'pietas'?
choices: Personal piety in a narrow religious sense, similar to modern Christian devotion.
Dutiful loyalty owed simultaneously to the gods, one's family (especially parents), and one's fatherland.
Mercy shown to defeated enemies in war.
Skill in performing public sacrifice.
questions.correctText: Dutiful loyalty owed simultaneously to the gods, one's family (especially parents), and one's fatherland.
questions.explanation: Roman pietas is broader than English 'piety.' It is the constellation of obligations binding a Roman to gods (cult, sacrifice, oath-keeping), family (especially father, ancestors, and children), and patria. Aeneas embodies all three: he obeys the gods, carries his father Anchises, leads his son Iulus, and founds Rome's future.

questions.prompt: What are the 'di penates' in Roman religion?
choices: Gods of war who march with the legions.
Household gods of the inner storeroom (penus), protecting the family's food and well-being; Aeneas carries them from Troy.
Gods of the underworld who escort the dead.
Civic gods worshipped only in the Roman Forum.
questions.correctText: Household gods of the inner storeroom (penus), protecting the family's food and well-being; Aeneas carries them from Troy.
questions.explanation: The Penates protect the household's stores (penus = pantry, inner storehouse), alongside the Lares (spirits of the place). In Aeneid 2, Anchises carries the Trojan Penates as he is borne on Aeneas's shoulders — symbolically transferring the religious life of Troy to the eventual site of Rome.

questions.prompt: In the Roman Republic of Caesar's day, what were the TWO supreme annually elected magistrates who jointly held imperium and command of the legions?
choices: The two censors
The two consuls
The two praetors
The two tribunes of the plebs
questions.correctText: The two consuls
questions.explanation: Two consuls, elected annually, held supreme civilian and military authority (imperium). Each could veto the other (a built-in check). Censors (every 5 years) oversaw the census and morals; praetors handled justice; tribunes of the plebs protected plebeian interests (10 of them, with veto power, but no imperium). Caesar was consul in 59 BCE before his Gallic command.

questions.prompt: How is a Roman legion of Caesar's era typically organized?
choices: Into 5 cohorts of 200 men each, commanded by tribunes.
Into 10 cohorts, each containing 6 centuries of (nominally) 80 men, led by centurions; the senior centurion of the first cohort is the primus pilus.
Into 3 maniples of 1000 men each, led by consuls.
Into 20 turmae of cavalry, with no infantry component.
questions.correctText: Into 10 cohorts, each containing 6 centuries of (nominally) 80 men, led by centurions; the senior centurion of the first cohort is the primus pilus.
questions.explanation: By Caesar's day a legion had 10 cohorts (cohortes); each cohort had 6 centuries (centuriae); each century was nominally 80 fighting men (the term 'centuria' is older, when it held closer to 100). Each century was led by a centurio; the primus pilus was the senior centurion of the first cohort and the legion's most senior centurion.

questions.prompt: In Caesar's ethnographic descriptions, what is the principal distinction he draws between Germani (across the Rhine) and Gauls?
choices: Germani are urbanized traders; Gauls are nomadic.

Germani have no Druids and no formal sacrifices, devote themselves to war and hunting, and (according to Caesar) hold no settled private land; Gauls are more sedentary, agrarian, and stratified with Druids and equites.
Germani worship Roman gods; Gauls do not.
Germani serve in the Roman legions; Gauls always oppose Rome.
questions.correctText: Germani have no Druids and no formal sacrifices, devote themselves to war and hunting, and (according to Caesar) hold no settled private land; Gauls are more sedentary, agrarian, and stratified with Druids and equites.
questions.explanation: In BG 6, Caesar contrasts the two peoples ethnographically: Gauls have an organized priestly caste (Druids), a knightly class (equites), elaborate ritual (including the wicker-man sacrifice), and settled agriculture. The Germani, he claims, have none of these — they reassign land yearly, focus on warfare and hunting, and worship only what they can see (sun, fire, moon). The contrast serves Caesar's literary and political purposes as much as ethnographic accuracy.
